planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; holly fern is one of the most drought-tolerant of all ferns and one of the best for Gulf Coast shade gardens — tolerating clay soil, drought, and considerable heat.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — holly fern is the most drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant of the widely grown shade ferns and one of the finest ferns for Gulf Coast gardens. The bold, lustrous, dark green fronds with glossy, sickle-shaped (falcate) pinnae have an unmistakably bold, coarse texture reminiscent of holly leaves — giving the fern its common name. It is essentially evergreen through zone 6–11, maintaining handsome fronds year-round even through light freezes. Unlike most ferns it tolerates dry spells between watering and grows in relatively dry soil conditions once established. Excellent for shade planting under trees where soil competition and drought would stress more delicate ferns. An outstanding and low-maintenance fern for southern shade gardens in zones 6–11.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; soft shield fern requires good drainage and will not tolerate waterlogged soil, but needs consistent moisture during its growing season.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — soft shield fern is considered by many gardeners the most beautiful of all the commonly grown shade ferns, producing extraordinarily finely divided fronds with a uniquely soft, feathery texture that distinguishes it from coarser ferns. The fronds are twice-pinnate with neatly rounded, finely crenate pinnules that give the whole frond an almost lacy appearance. Many named cultivars exist with even more divided fronds ('Divisilobum Group'), cristate (crested) tips, or plumose forms. Unlike many Polystichum species, soft shield fern is semi-evergreen in zone 7–9, holding its fronds through winter to provide year-round interest. The soft, mounding clumps combine beautifully with hostas, astilbe, and woodland wildflowers. Excellent for the shaded border in zones 5–9.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs or hardwood cuttings in late winter or spring in any moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; blue elderberry is far more drought and alkaline-soil tolerant than American elderberry (S. canadensis) and thrives in western conditions.

directions: Plant in any moist to well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade — blue elderberry is the western North American native elderberry, native from British Columbia to Baja California and east to the Rocky Mountains, producing large, flat-topped clusters of dark blue to blue-black berries with a distinctive waxy, blue-gray bloom (giving both plant and fruit their names). The berries are slightly sweeter than American elderberry (S. canadensis) and are used the same way — in jellies, juices, wines, elderflower cordial, and traditional medicine. Blue elderberry is significantly more drought-tolerant than American elderberry, adapts to alkaline soils, and grows in the dry, sunny conditions of western landscapes where the eastern species struggles. It is an important food and medicine plant in the traditions of dozens of western Native American nations. Plant where spreading by suckers is acceptable.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; Chinese wolfberry spreads by roots and can become invasive in suitable soils — plant in a contained area or where spreading is acceptable.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade — Chinese wolfberry is similar to goji berry (L. barbarum) but more cold-hardy and shade-tolerant, producing red-orange elongated berries with the same sweet-astringent, tart-sweet flavor. In traditional Chinese medicine, the leaves (often cooked as a vegetable), young shoots, and berries are all used. The dried berries (gou qi zi) are a fundamental ingredient in Chinese tonic soups and herbal preparations, used for eye health and as a general tonic for centuries. Fresh berries are also edible and pleasant. The arching, thornless-to-spiny stems produce attractive light purple flowers in summer before the berries. Remarkably adaptable to difficult soils including alkaline, clay, and dry soils, and more shade-tolerant than most full-sun fruiting shrubs. Note that it spreads by roots and seeds; manage accordingly.

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in fall in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry-to-average sandy or rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; triplet lily is dormant and completely dry in summer — do not water during summer dormancy or the corms will rot.

directions: Plant corms in fall in very well-drained, dry-to-average, sandy or rocky soil in full sun to partial shade — triplet lily is a spectacular western native bulb of California and Oregon grasslands and open woodlands, producing umbels of 15–30 funnel-shaped blue-violet to purple flowers (2 inches long) on 18–24 inch stems in late spring to early summer. The flowers are among the bluest of all native American wildflowers and provide important nectar for native bumblebees and long-tongued bees. The plant is entirely summer-dormant — it emerges in early spring, blooms, and then disappears as summer approaches, requiring dry summer conditions for healthy corm development (do not irrigate in summer). Excellent for naturalizing in dry, warm-summer climates in well-drained rocky or sandy soils — it multiplies steadily by corm offsets in appropriate conditions. An outstanding native bulb for Mediterranean-climate and dry western gardens.
